name: Present tense (nutid)
explanation: Danish uses the present tense (nutid) to describe habits and things that happen regularly — perfect for talking about your daily routine. Unlike English, the verb form is the same for every subject: 'jeg arbejder', 'du arbejder', 'han arbejder'. There is no separate '-s' form for he/she.

examples.danish: Jeg vågner klokken syv hver dag.
examples.english: I wake up at seven o'clock every day.

name: Regular verb conjugation
explanation: Most Danish verbs are regular: take the infinitive (the 'at' form) and add '-r' to make the present tense. So 'at spise' becomes 'spiser', 'at arbejde' becomes 'arbejder', and 'at vågne' becomes 'vågner'. The same form is used for all subjects.

examples.english: We eat breakfast at eight o'clock.
They sleep late at the weekend.

name: Main clause word order (SVO)
explanation: A Danish statement follows Subject-Verb-Object order, just like English. Frequency adverbs such as 'altid', 'ofte', 'aldrig' and 'sjældent' come right after the verb: 'Jeg spiser altid morgenmad'. Time expressions like 'om morgenen' usually go at the end of the sentence.

examples.danish: Jeg drikker altid kaffe om morgenen.
examples.english: I always drink coffee in the morning.

examples.danish: Han ser aldrig fjernsyn om aftenen.
examples.english: He never watches TV in the evening.
